label: Game 8, mid-turn 1, after 13 hand-card placements; board is now CLEAN. Player hits Hint with hand [5C' 2D 7D']. The current engine hint is judged 'too complicated' for a clean-board state.
continues_from: in_progress/dirty_board_example.json (which captured state after action 9; this captures state after action 13)

actions_since_dirty_board_example.produces: [6S' 7S' 8S']  — pure_run length-3 helper, the dangling 8S' consolidated onto its partial

current_engine_hint: yank 6S from HELPER [2C 3D 4C 5H 6S 7H], absorb onto [7D'] → [6S 7D'] ; spawn [7H]

structural_complexity: yank from the MIDDLE of a length-6 helper — a destructive split of a working stack
the yank's spawned [7H] then has to be re-pushed onto a different helper before it loses helper status
the new [6S 7D'] partial is then pushed onto what's left of the original helper, rebuilding it as a different shape
current_engine_hint_notes.human_feel: The player has to tear apart [2C 3D 4C 5H 6S 7H] mid-stream, juggle the spawned 7H, and reassemble. Three structurally independent reorganizations for one 7D' placement. High cognitive load even though the BFS plan-length is shorter than the dirty-board case (3 vs 4).

open_questions: On a clean board, is there a play involving any of [5C' 2D 7D'] that the engine would judge longer in BFS steps but a human would find structurally simpler? (E.g., a play that adds to one helper without tearing another apart.)
Should the cost function include a 'helpers disturbed' term so a clean stay-out-of-the-way play wins ties over a yank-and-rebuild play?

On a clean board with a small hand (3 cards here), is one of the right choices 'don't play this turn' — i.e., the optimal hint can be empty? Today findPlay forces a placement; the hand-aware contract assumes you want one.
Is 7D' fundamentally harder to play than 5C' or 2D from this board state? If yes, would a human prefer to be told 'try something else' rather than 'do this elaborate yank'?

steves_actual_solution.summary: Two UI motions, structurally simpler than the engine's hint, and BFS-invisible by design.
ui_motions: Drag 7H from the right end of HELPER [2C 3D 4C 5H 6S 7H] onto HELPER [7S 7D 7C]. UI realizes as split @ 5 + merge_stack [7H] -> [7S 7D 7C], producing [2C 3D 4C 5H 6S] + [7S 7D 7C 7H].
Drag 7D' from hand onto HELPER [2C 3D 4C 5H 6S] /right, producing [2C 3D 4C 5H 6S 7D'].
steves_actual_solution.verbatim_actions_in_log: session 8 actions 24, 25, 26 (after 17–23 'refresh' noise that resets the previous play).
steves_actual_solution.why_bfs_doesnt_find_this: The first move is a HELPER → HELPER rearrangement. The BFS verb library (extract+absorb, free_pull, push, splice, shift, decompose) only emits moves that touch trouble or growing — every frontier expansion commits to reducing trouble. A pure helper-to-helper card transfer with no trouble involved isn't a verb. By design: it keeps the search space tractable. But it permanently misses the class of plays where ONE cheap rearrangement makes the rest of the turn a single push.
steves_actual_solution.deeper_principle: Steve's play exploits exactly that gap. Helper-only setup moves are 'free' from a trouble-reduction perspective but they unlock single-step finishes. To find such plans, the engine would need either (a) a verb that captures helper-helper transfers, or (b) a pre-pass that considers small helper rearrangements as setup before launching the main BFS, or (c) some recognition pattern (e.g. 'this hand card would slot into a length-N run if its current end-card were elsewhere — try moving the end-card first').
